summary: Ru'yat Allah is the theological question of whether God can be seen, especially by the blessed in the hereafter. It was a classic point of dispute in kalam, with the Mu'tazila denying the visibility of God and the Ash'ariyya and other Sunni schools affirming it.
description:
The question of ru'yat Allah asks whether God may be perceived by sight, most centrally whether the righteous will see God in the world to come. Its scriptural touchstones include Qur'anic verses read by proponents as promising the vision (notably 75:22–23, "faces that day shall be radiant, looking toward their Lord") and by opponents as denying it (notably 6:103, "sight cannot reach Him"), together with the episode of Moses at 7:143.

The Mu'tazila denied that God can be seen, in this life or the next. On their account, whatever is seen must occupy a direction and place and stand in a spatial relation to the viewer; since God is not a body and is not localized in space, ascribing visibility to Him would compromise His transcendence (tanzih). Verses appearing to promise the vision were accordingly read figuratively, for example, taking "looking toward their Lord" as awaiting His reward.

Sunni theologians affirmed the vision. Ash'ari, Maturidi, Hanbali, and related theologians held that the blessed will see God in the hereafter, taking the affirming verses and reports at face value while denying that this seeing implies that God is a body, occupies a direction, or is comprehended in His essence. A common formulation affirms the vision "without modality" (bila kayf) and "without likening" (bila tashbih), so that the reality of the vision is granted without conceding an anthropomorphic account of how it occurs. On this affirmation the Ash'ari, Maturidi, and Hanbali currents stood together against the Mu'tazili denial, a convergence traced in the scholarly sources cited below (Gimaret's reconstruction of al-Ash'ari, and Rahmadani and Eriza on the Maturidi position), so that the dispute over the vision runs primarily along a single Sunni-versus-Mu'tazili axis rather than dividing the Sunni schools among themselves.
editorial_notes: This entry presents the two principal poles of the classical dispute, Mu'tazili denial and the affirmation shared across the Sunni theological schools. Per neutrality principle #9 the reference-work citations were removed (Gimaret's Encyclopaedia of Islam entry on the vision of God, and the Routledge Encyclopedia of Philosophy survey of Ashʿariyya and Muʿtazila); in their place Daniel Gimaret's academic monograph La doctrine d'al-Ashʿarī (Cerf, 1990) was added, which reconstructs al-Ashʿarī's affirmation of the vision against the Muʿtazilī denial and so supports both poles. It does not adjudicate the exegesis of the cited Qur'anic verses; the specific verse references are given as the texts around which the debate is conventionally organized, and the schools' opposed readings are attributed, not endorsed, per the neutrality policy. Internal variation, e.g., among Ash'aris on the precise mechanism of the vision, or distinct Shi'i and Ibadi positions, is out of scope here and noted as a candidate for expansion. Verse translations are the editor's plain renderings for orientation, not authoritative translations. The verse references (Q 75:22-23, 6:103, 7:143) were checked on 2026-07-18 against the Saheeh International translation (via the Quranic Arabic Corpus, corpus.quran.com): 75:22-23, '[Some] faces, that Day, will be radiant, / Looking at their Lord'; 6:103, 'Vision perceives Him not, but He perceives [all] vision'; 7:143, the Sinai episode in which Moses asks 'My Lord, show me [Yourself] that I may look at You' and is answered 'You will not see Me'. The plain renderings in the description track these and were left as orientation paraphrases; the orphaned meta-phrase 'reference literature notes...', a leftover from removed reference works, was rewritten into neutral exposition attributing the Sunni/Muʿtazilī alignment to the surviving scholarly sources (Gimaret; Rahmadani and Eriza). Muslim/Islamic scholarly primary texts have been added for triangulation and balanced coverage (neutrality principles #7 and #8): al-Ashʿarī's al-Ibāna and al-Ṭaḥāwī's al-ʿAqīda al-Ṭaḥāwiyya, both affirming the vision in the hereafter without modality/resemblance from within the tradition; cited at medium confidence as their relevant passages were not read line-by-line. Third-lens update (2026-07-18): a modern Muslim scholarly source was appended, Rahmadani and Eriza's peer-reviewed article Kalam Maturidi and the Concept of God (Pharos Journal of Theology 106, no. 5, 2025), which explicitly analyzes ruʾyat Allāh as a Māturīdī affirmation of the vision of God in the hereafter and sets it against the Ashʿarī and Muʿtazilī positions; verified via the DOAJ record and the journal's 2025 index (article 31, vol. 106, issue 5) and the article's own abstract. The entry now triangulates three independent lenses that converge on the same map of the dispute (Sunni affirmation without modality versus Muʿtazilī denial): Western academic Islamic studies (Gimaret), classical Muslim primary texts (al-Ashʿarī, al-Ṭaḥāwī), and a contemporary Muslim scholarly article (Rahmadani and Eriza); the article was rated medium when only its abstract and index had been consulted. Verified-tier pass (2026-07-19): the Rahmadani and Eriza article was read in full from the publisher's open PDF (pharosjot.com; DOI 10.46222/pharosjot.106.531). Confirmed against the text actually read: al-Maturidi affirms that 'the vision of God (ru'yat Allah) is a genuine possibility and that it will indeed take place on the Day of Resurrection', drawing on Q 75:22-23 (article p. 7); the article states that 'the form of the vision ... is not susceptible to rational demonstration nor to a literalist interpretation that treats it as the apprehension of a material entity' and that its modality is left to divine inscrutability (p. 7); and it explicitly sets this affirmation against the Mu'tazili denial ('This position stands in sharp relief to the Mu'tazili stance, which typically dismisses similar propositions', p. 7), while noting the Maturidi bila-kayf-style refusal to demand a rational elucidation of the 'how'. This carries the entry's Maturidi pole and the Maturidi-versus-Mu'tazili axis, and the source is raised to high confidence accordingly. STILL UNVERIFIED against sources actually read, so the entry remains 'reviewed' rather than 'verified': (1) the Ash'ari affirmation of the vision and Gimaret's reconstruction of al-Ashari's position (Gimaret, La doctrine d'al-Ashari, was not read line-by-line); (2) the Hanbali affirmation, which the Pharos article does not address, and which rests on al-Ibana and al-Tahawiyya (cited but not read line-by-line here). The article's own comparison table (Table 1, pp. 8-9) covers attributes, kasb, kalam Allah, obligations, major sins and the role of reason, but has no ru'yat-Allah row for the Ash'ari or Hanbali columns, so it does not independently confirm the tri-school Sunni convergence asserted in the description. The Qur'anic verse references (Q 75:22-23, 6:103, 7:143) were checked on 2026-07-18 against the Saheeh International translation via corpus.quran.com and are confirmed; Q 75:22-23 is also quoted by the Pharos article as read.

relations.note: The Mu'tazili denial of the vision rests on safeguarding God's transcendence.
Whether God has 'sight' and can be an object of sight belongs to the debate over the divine attributes.
Affirmers frame the vision so as to avoid likening God to a visible body.
Ash'ari defended the visibility of God against the Mu'tazila.

historical_period.note: A standing question of classical kalam from roughly the 8th century CE, sharpened in the Mu'tazili–Ash'ari controversies of the 9th–10th centuries.

sources.citation: Rahmadani, S., and Faisal Eriza. "Kalam Maturidi and the Concept of God: A Theological Analysis from Classical and Contemporary Islamic Perspectives." Pharos Journal of Theology 106, no. 5 (2025): article 31. Peer-reviewed article by contemporary Muslim scholars examining the Māturīdī doctrine of God, including ruʾyat Allāh (the vision of God) as a real event in the hereafter not reducible to a material apprehension, set in comparison with the Ashʿarī and Muʿtazilī positions.
sources.license: Open access (DOAJ-indexed peer-reviewed journal); full text read from the publisher's open PDF (2026-07-19).

sources.citation: Gimaret, Daniel. La doctrine d'al-Ashʿarī. Paris: Éditions du Cerf, 1990. Academic monograph giving a systematic reconstruction of al-Ashʿarī's theology, including his affirmation of the vision of God (ruʾyat Allāh) in the hereafter against the Muʿtazilī denial.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference, consulted via bibliographic record, not read in full.

sources.citation: al-Ashʿarī, Abū al-Ḥasan. al-Ibāna ʿan Uṣūl al-Diyāna. English trans. W. C. Klein, The Elucidation of Islam's Foundation, American Oriental Society, New Haven, 1940. Foundational Ashʿarī creed affirming that God will be seen in the hereafter without resemblance, against the Muʿtazilī denial.
sources.license: Public-domain classical text; 1940 translation cited for reference; relevant passages not read line-by-line.

sources.citation: al-Ṭaḥāwī, Abū Jaʿfar Aḥmad. al-ʿAqīda al-Ṭaḥāwiyya (The Creed of al-Ṭaḥāwī). Widely translated classical Sunni creed affirming the reality of the beatific vision of God in the hereafter 'without modality' (bilā kayf).
sources.license: Public-domain classical text; cited for reference; relevant passages not read line-by-line.
